coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis and photoelectrical performance of feather-like tellurium on nickel foil
coredata.dc:creator: Mu, Yannan
Gao, Xiaocheng
Zhou, Xiaoming
Wang, Haiyan
Lv, Pin
Qiu, Min
Zhang, Xiuping
coredata.dc:description: Feather-like tellurium (Te) was synthesized on Ni substrates by the one-step electrodeposition method no external agents. The morphology, structure, optical and photoelectricity properties of the as-prepared Te film were investigated in detail. The results revealed that the feather-like Te film with a preferred (110) orientation demonstrated hexagonal structure and strong light absorption in the visible range. Te and Ni formed low resistance Ohmic contact; the conductivity of feather-like Te on Ni foil (S3) was measured to be 20.63 × 10−5 s/cm. In addition, the photocurrent density of as-prepared feather-like Te (S3) measured to be 0.0170 mA/cm2, nearly increased by 77% than lowest S4 (0.0039 mA/cm2). This indicates feather-like Te has potential desirable applications in photoelectric devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrodeposition
Tellurium
Feather-like
Photoelectricity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the University Nursing Program for Young Scholars with Creative Talents in Heilongjiang Province (UNPYSCT- 2015114 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (No. 2017001 ), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Superhard Materials (Jilin University) (No. 201713 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61504002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Experimental schematic diagram of the apparatus for Te film by electrodeposition method; (b) Cyclic voltammogram on Ni substrate in the electrolyte solution containing 0.015 mol L−1 Na2TeO3 and 1 mol L−1 HNO3.
XRD pattern of Te samples on Ni substrate: (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4.
FESEM images of Te films electrodeposited at different potentials for 300 s: (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4.
UV–visible absorption spectra of Te samples: (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4.
Current voltage (I–V) curves of Te/Ni samples (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4. Inset: the structure schematic diagram of electrical properties measure.
Reversible switching photoelectrical property of Te/Ni samples: (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis and photoelectrical performance of feather-like tellurium on nickel foil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Heihe University, Heihe 164300, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin 132022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Physical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Polarization and Information Technology, Qufu Normal University, 273165, Qufu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Physical Engineering
Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Polarization and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Feather-like tellurium (Te) was synthesized on Ni substrates by the one-step electrodeposition method no external agents. The morphology, structure, optical and photoelectricity properties of the as-prepared Te film were investigated in detail. The results revealed that the feather-like Te film with a preferred (110) orientation demonstrated hexagonal structure and strong light absorption in the visible range. Te and Ni formed low resistance Ohmic contact; the conductivity of feather-like Te on Ni foil (S3) was measured to be 20.63 × 10−5 s/cm. In addition, the photocurrent density of as-prepared feather-like Te (S3) measured to be 0.0170 mA/cm2, nearly increased by 77% than lowest S4 (0.0039 mA/cm2). This indicates feather-like Te has potential desirable applications in photoelectric devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Tellurium (Te) as a p-type semiconductor has been extensively studied, owing to its unique and useful properties such as nonlinear optical responses, thermoelectricity, piezoelectricity, gas sensing, high theoretical volumetric capacity, photoconductivity and photoelectricity [1–7]. In addition, Te can be used as chemical transformation template, cathode for high-energy Li–Te batteries and an effective anticancer agent [8–10]. Over the last decades, many researchers focused on fabricating CdTe solar cells on metal substrates. The metals that received substantial attention are Ni, Mo, Ti and stainless steel [11–14]. Flexible CdTe solar cells on metallic substrates can be fabricated in a roll-to-roll process; it is expected to lead to a significant price reduction of CdTe solar cells. Ni may match the work function of CdTe (4.7 eV) to form Ohimic contact [15]. It is well known that Te can easily react with some elements to generate new functional materials such as Ag2Te, Bi2Te3, HgCdTe and CdTe [16–19], and a small amount of doping can lead to significant changes in physical properties [20]. In the process of heat treatment processing, the Te element of CdTe may react with metal Ni meaning it is Cd-rich. In CdTe/CdS solar cells, expected p-type CdTe is Te-rich [21,22]. Hence, preparing Te on Ni substrates as the back contact layer for CdTe solar cell is necessary.

Novel nanostructure materials have recently attracted attention due to their unique properties different from the bulk material [23–29]. Nanostructured Te is an emerging functional material. At present, nanostructured Te including quantum dots, nanoparticles, nanorods, nanowires, nanobelts, nanosheets, nanotubes, nanoplates, tree-like, feather-like, flower-like and chiral nanostructures have been synthesized by various preparation methods like electrochemical deposition, solvothermal method, the reflux process, ultrasonicinduced growth, physical vapor deposition, hydrothermal method, the sublimation–condensation process, the photo-thermally assisted method, microwave-assisted synthesis and template based strategies [30–42]. The electrodeposition of nanostructured Te has attracted great interested due to its low energy consumption, high material incorporation and simple technique. For instance, Te nanowires were fabricated using an anodic aluminum membrane (AAM) with an Au layer as a template [46]. In addition, Szymczak et al. deposited the single crystalline Te nanowires on a Pt disk in a piperidinium-based ionic liquid mixture: 1-ethyl-1-octylpiperidinium bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide [41]. Bartlett et al. used tellurium salt ([NnBu4]2[TeCl6]) to electrodeposit Te nanowires into porous AAO membranes [47]. In this paper, we present a simple, environmental friendly and template-free one-step electrodeposition technique to prepare feather-like Te film on Ni substrates at room temperature. It is worth noting that there is no external agent in the electrolyte solution. The morphology, structure and photoelectrical properties of the feather-like Te film have been investigated. The photoelectrical properties of the feather-like Te film deposited on Ni indicate its potential application in solar cells.

The experiments are carried out in a three-electrode cell in air. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), a graphite plate, an Ag/AgCl electrode and a cleaned Ni foil (2.5 × 4 cm2) were used as the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode, respectively. In our experiment, the electrolytic solution consists of 0.015 mol L−1 sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3) and 1 mol L−1 nitric acid (HNO3). All of the chemicals are analytical grade.

Cyclic voltammogram (CV) was used to study the electrochemical reactions in the electrolyte solution (0.015 mol L−1 Na2TeO3 + 1 mol L−1 HNO3) at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 1(b), one cathodic peak was observed, which indicates the formation of Te on Ni foils. In our experiment, when the potential is scanned in the negative potential direction, a visible gray film appears on the Ni substrate around −0.28 V, which is due to the reduction of TeO3 2−, corresponding to the following equation:
TeO
3
2 −
+
6 H
+
+
4 e
−
→ Te +
3 H
2
O

CV provides us a reference. Therefore, the applied potentials are at −0.25 V, −0.28 V, −0.31 V and −0.34 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in the experiment. The thin films are deposited on Ni foil for 300 s at room temperature. As-prepared samples are labeled as S1, S2, S3 and S4, respectively. As-deposited Te samples are rinsed with deionized water and dried with N2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was obtained by a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer range from 20° to 80°. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs were obtained by a model JEOL JSM-6700 field emission scanning electron microscope. A UV-3150 double beam spectrophotometer was used to analyze the optical absorption spectra. The electronic properties of as-prepared samples were characterized by Keithley 2400 Source Meter in air. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties were investigated using an electrochemical workstation (CHI601C). Ag/AgCl was used as a reference electrode. Pt mesh and as-prepared samples were used as the counter electrode and work electrode, respectively. Sunlight was simulated with an intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at room temperature.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns for the samples. As shown in Fig. 2, the diffraction peaks from the Ni substrate are marked with “&”. The peaks of samples appear at 23.34°, 27.96°, 40.90°, 47.51°, 64.25° and 66.32° which can correspond to the (100), (101), (110), (220), (210) and (211) planes of hexagonal phase Te, as confirmed by the JCPDS X-ray powder file data (36-1452). No other phase is observed for impurities indicating pure Te is obtained. The diffraction peaks are sharp, which indicate that the Te films are well crystallized.

The morphology of the Te film is confirmed by FESEM. The results reveal that the electrodeposited potential plays an important role in the formation of the various microstructures. As shown in Fig. 3 , the morphology exhibits obvious differences. In Fig. 3(a), the as-prepared Te forms nearly feather-like Te nanostructure when the electrodeposited potential is −0.25 V. The more negative potential leads to an abundance of tellurium in the electrolytic solution, which results in rapid precipitation and deposition [43]. In Fig. 3(b), the full feather-like structures are obtained. Such precipitation and fast deposition may cause many defects which act as nuclei for further growth to feather-like structures [44]. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the length of feather-like Te is not uniform, which is as long as tens of microns. The Te feather is composed of a long central trunk and many highly ordered secondary branches. Further observation shows that the secondary branches are composed of well-oriented tapered nanorods with length up to several micrometers and diameters of hundreds of nanometers. The secondary branches are parallel to each other and emerge at the same angles with respect to the corresponding central trunk [37]. As-prepared feather-like Te nanostructure is similar to the morphology reported by Wang et al. via a biphasic interfacial reaction route [37]. A substantial change in the morphology of Te is observed when the electrodeposited potential is further decreased to −0.34 V. In Fig. 3 (d), we can see that many nanorods and composite structures with different lengths are distributed randomly. The deposited potential plays an important role to implant seeds which grow branches in every orientation to form a different morphology of Te films.

The optical properties of nano-materials are drastically different from those of bulk materials [44,45]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the as-prepared Te films exhibit obvious light absorption characteristics in the visible region. Moreover, it can be found that the absorption ability strengthens first and then weakens with the deposited potentials becoming more negative. In Fig. 4(a–c), we can see absorption peaks (around 563 nm), which can be attributed to a forbidden direction transition [38,48]. Among all of the films, the extensive feather-like Te films exhibit the highest absorption when the deposited potential is −0.31 V, which is mainly due to its excellent light-scattering ability. The ability to absorb visible light is related to the morphology type. The shape of the feather could scatter incident light several times, and the scattering frequency could increase the absorption [49–52]. In Fig. 4(d), the intensity of the absorption band becomes noticeably weaker when the deposited potential is −0.34 V. The ability to absorb visible light indicates that feather-like Te film has a great application in photovoltaic devices. However, the exact luminescent mechanism needs to be further investigated.

When a metal forms a contact with a semiconductor, there are two type contacts at the metal-semiconductor (M-S) interface: Schottky contact (rectifying contact) and Ohmic contact (non-rectifying contact) [53,54]. Schottky contact indicates the potential barrier formed mainly due to the misalignment of the work function between metal and semiconductor. Schottky contact hinders charge transport and causes a high contact resistance at the junction. In photovoltaic devices, Schottky contact lowers short-circuit current and photoelectric conversion efficiency [55]; it should be avoided or reduced. Conversely, Ohmic contact between metal and semiconductor is defined by the negligible contact resistance relative to the bulk and spreading resistance of the semiconductor, which is beneficial to the travel of photo-generated carriers [56,57]. Therefore, Ohmic contact with low contact resistance is desired in photovoltaic devices. To characterize the conductive performance of feather-like Te, colloidal silver paste was used as conductive medium to contact copper wire and Te/Ni thin films. The schematic diagram is shown in the inset of Fig. 5 . The current-voltage (I-V) curves of Te films deposited at different potentials are shown in Fig. 5. It is well known that Ohmic contact is a linear and symmetrical current-voltage characteristic curve on a semiconductor device [56,57]. Fig. 5 shows that all the currents increase linearly with increasing voltage. The linear behavior confirms that the contact of Te film with Ni substrates is Ohmic contact. According to first-principles calculations done by Liu et al., the valence-band minimum (VBM) energies of various Te thickness are relatively high ranging from −5 to −4.67 eV [58]. Ni has a large work function (5.15 eV) [15]. It is higher than VBM energies of Te, which suggests a lower barrier for holes injection transport. Thus, the formation of Ohmic contact between Ni foil and Te films can be attributed to the matching of work function and Fermi energies. The slope of the characterization line in Fig. 5 is the conductance. A sharper slope means greater conductance of samples. It is obvious that the conductance of as-prepared samples increases first (S1 to S3) and then decreases (S4). It was highly insulating when deposited at −0.34 V, with electrical conductivity of 1.25 × 10−5 s/cm. The conductivity of S3 (20.63 × 10−5 s/cm) is higher than other samples, which is a good match with Figs. 2 and 3. It is well known better crystallized results in fewer less defects. As shown in Fig. 2, the diffraction peaks of S3 are sharp, which indicates that Te films are well crystallized. As shown in Fig. 3, the film surface continuity of S3 is better than other samples. More film surface continuity on the substrate leads to more electrical conductivity of S3, which benefits charge transporting. The results indicate that the feather-like Te deposited on Ni possess better Ohmic contact characteristics.

The PEC characteristic of the feather-like Te film is investigated at room temperature in a conventional three-electrode system. A mixture of 0.25 mol L−1 Na2S and 0.35 mol L−1 Na2SO3 aqueous solution is used as the electrolyte. This polysulfide (Sn2−) redox mediator is known to form charge-separating junctions with Te and suppress photo-oxidation. When the as-prepared sample is irradiated by the simulated solar light, the diffusion of photo-generated carriers (electrons and holes) occurs due to the different charge concentration, and electron–hole pairs would separate in the space charge region. The positive and negative charges transport to Te/Ni and Pt mesh, respectively, forming the photocurrent in circuit. Fig. 6 shows the photocurrent density as function of time measured with and without lamp illumination. As shown in Fig. 6, there is no significant current is observed in the dark (without illumination). The photocurrent density ramps to a high value when the light was turned on from the dark. The photocurrent density of the samples decreased faintly over time, similar to other photoelectrical materials [59]. The photocurrent density then approximately reaches a steady state. In our experiments, the device fabricated using feather-like Te shows a strong response to light (Js3≈0.0170 mA/cm2). Conversely, Te film without feather-like structure (S4) shows weak but detectable response to sunlight (Js4≈0.0039 mA/cm2). Further optimization of the devices is ongoing. The better photovoltaic properties should be attributed to better crystallization and photo absorption. In addition, the electrical properties of S3 reduce the contact resistance with Ni foil. The weaker resistance improves the charge mobility. The connectivity of Te and Ni substrate affects the photoelectrical property. The morphology of feather-like Te can be divided into two parts, trunk (central section) and side section with a parallel rod array structure. The trunk is similar to the rachis of a feather formed on substrate, which supplies a pathway for the photo-generated charge carriers to travel to Ni foil [60]. The side section is similar to the barbules of a feather serving as a light scattering layer for superior light harvesting efficiency [50]. In other words, strong light absorption and direct migration path result in more electrons concentrated into the center of the feather-like Te to be transported. Overall, the two parts of feather-like Te improve of the photoelectrical properties.

In this paper, we prepared ultra-long feather-like Te film on Ni substrate by electrodeposition method with no external agent. A feather-like Te can be obtained at −0.31 V for 300 s. XRD patterns show that all as-prepared Te thin films are hexagonal. The optical study results show that feather-like Te film have intense absorption in the visible region. As-prepared feather-like Te film shows low resistance Ohmic contact with Ni and strong response to light. In addition, this work could be a reference for the fabrication of Te nanostructures by the template-free electrodeposition method.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the University Nursing Program for Young Scholars with Creative Talents in Heilongjiang Province (UNPYSCT-2015114), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (No. 2017001), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Superhard Materials (Jilin University) (No. 201713) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61504002).
